Dota 2 - игра, которая одновременно является бесплатной, но стоит тебе жизни, психики, отношений, сна и, иногда, клавиатуры  Я начал играть в доту как человек. У меня были цели, амбиции, друзья, нормальный режим сна и вера в человечество.  Сейчас у меня осталась только ненависть к туману войны и привычка проверять миникарту даже когда я смотрю порно.   ПЕРВОЕ ЗНАКОМСТВО  Я помню свой первый матч.  Меня встретили 9 человек, которые уже явно закончили минимум по три университета по токсичности, психологии давления и искусству унижения новичков.  Я выбрал героя, нажал “рандом”, и игра сказала: «Поздравляем, ты теперь саппорт без понимания, что ты саппорт».  Мой керри написал:   “удали игру уёбок”   Я ещё не знал, что это не совет. Это была философия.   ОБУЧЕНИЕ  Обучение в доту это когда тебе объясняют, как играть, а потом бросают в лес, как ребёнка в экономику.  «Купи варды»  Купил варды  Поставил варды  Умер, потому что поставил варды  Керри: “репорт уёбку на саппе”   Логика ясна. Варды нужны, чтобы ты видел момент своей смерти в пиздатом качестве.   РАНГОВАЯ СИСТЕМА  ММР - это не рейтинг.  Это психологический эксперимент валв над тем, как долго человек может верить, что он поднимется, прежде чем начнёт пикать пуджа из принципа.  Каждый матч - это:  Один человек, который играет как TI победитель  Один человек, который только что проснулся  Один человек, который играет ногами  И я, который пытается понять, почему мы все в одной команде   ГЕЙМПЛЕЙ  Dota 2 - это единственная игра, где:  ты можешь выиграть 45 минут  и проиграть за 7 секунд, потому что кто-то пошёл бить древних без байбека, трезвости и совести   Каждая драка выглядит так:  1. Кто-то кричит «го выебем их ща»   2. Половина команды не готова   3. Вторая половина уже умерла   4. Я нажимаю телепорт на таверну, которого уже нет   СОЮЗНИКИ  Союзники в доте - это не команда.  Это 4 незнакомца, объединённые только общей болью и отсутствием взаимопонимания.  Типы союзников:  Мидер: «Я смурф даблите» → 0/5 на 6-й минуте  Керри: фармит лес, пока несут трон  Саппорт: купил варды один раз и теперь философ  Оффлейнер: ушёл в лес, потому что  захотел   ВРАГИ  Враги всегда:  знают тайминги  читают карту  любят тебя  особенно когда ты на 10 HP и без телепорта   ОБЩЕНИЕ  Чат в доте — это место, где:  советы выглядят как угрозы  угрозы выглядят как религия  и каждый второй — будущий участник судебного разбирательства, если бы валв добавила суд   ПОБЕДЫ  Иногда ты выигрываешь.  Это происходит случайно, примерно как найти деньги в старой куртке.  Ты не понимаешь почему победил, команда не понимает почему победила, и никто не чувствует, что это заслуженно.   ПОРАЖЕНИЯ  Поражения — это не конец.  Это начало следующего матча, где ты уже морально сломан, но всё равно жмёшь поиск, потому что мозг уже подписал контракт с зависимостью.   ИТОГ  Dota 2 - это не игра.  Это:  психологический тренинг  симулятор коллективной ответственности без ответственности  школа терпения, злости и принятия неизбежного  и единственное место, где фраза “gg ez” звучит как объявление войны   Рекомендую.  Не потому что хорошая игра, а потому что уже поздно её удалять

I've been playing Dota 2 for so long that at this point I'm not entirely sure whether I play Dota or Dota plays me.  The weird thing is I don't even grind ranked anymore.  Turbo is where it's at.  My friends and I treat Dota 2 like that old reliable restaurant you've been going to for years. You know the menu. You know exactly what you're getting. You know you'll probably complain about it while you're there.  And yet somehow you keep coming back.  Every time a new game comes out, the group gets excited.  "We're definitely playing this for months."  Two weeks later the Discord call goes quiet.  Somebody eventually says the magic words:  "Dota?"  And just like that, ten years of gaming innovation gets thrown into a ditch.  Back to Turbo.  Back to chaos.  Back to five grown adults making terrible decisions at maximum speed.  That's what makes Turbo so good.  Nobody cares.  People are picking heroes they haven't touched in three years.  Someone is building items that should probably violate international law.  Your friend swears his build is "secret tech" even though it's currently 4-12 and being investigated by multiple human rights organizations.  And somehow it still works.  Sometimes.  The matches are fast enough that losing doesn't feel like you've wasted an evening.  You get stomped?  Whatever.  Queue again.  Twenty minutes later you're all laughing because a fully farmed support just deleted somebody from existence using what appears to be a kitchen utensil and raw confidence.  The best part isn't even the game anymore.  It's the people.  Dota has become less of a game and more of a digital pub where the activity is yelling at each other while occasionally remembering to destroy the Ancient.  Half the fun is listening to your friends confidently announce a terrible plan and then watching it unfold exactly as badly as expected.  The game itself is somehow still amazing after all these years.  Every match creates a different kind of stupidity.  Some games feel like a tactical war.  Others feel like five shopping carts crashing into each other in a supermarket parking lot.  Both are equally entertaining.  Sure, Dota has problems.  The learning curve is less of a curve and more of a vertical cliff covered in broken glass.  The community can occasionally make you question the future of humanity.  And every patch somehow manages to make heroes stronger, weaker, stronger again, then somehow turn a support into a raid boss.  But none of that really matters.  Because when the group has nothing new to play, Dota is always there.  Waiting.  Patiently.  Like an old friend.  Or a highly intelligent curse.  Right. Time to queue Turbo and immediately regret trusting my teammates with anything more complicated than breathing.

9.1 Overall Score  Dota 2 is less a game and more a discipline. It will humble you, exhaust you, make you question your decisions at 2am — and then reward you with moments of coordination so clean they feel like art. It is not for everyone, and it does not pretend to be. But for those willing to put in the hours, it's the most intellectually demanding competitive game available, and it's completely free. You don't play Dota. You endure it until you love it.

Rating: 10/10, would willingly ruin my mental health again.  Dota 2 is a free-to-play game developed by people who apparently asked themselves, "What if chess was played by five screaming strangers who all think they're military geniuses?"  The game starts simply enough. You pick a hero. Then within five minutes, you're being blamed for events that happened before you were born.  Missed one last hit? Trash.  Died once? Reported.  Won your lane? Doesn't matter. Your carry is currently farming the jungle while the enemy destroys your Ancient and typing, "gg team no support."  The learning curve of Dota 2 isn't a curve. It's a vertical concrete wall coated in cooking oil. New players enter a match and immediately discover there are 100+ heroes, hundreds of abilities, dozens of items, countless mechanics, and approximately 4 million ways to embarrass themselves in front of strangers.  The community is one of the greatest social experiments ever conducted. Nowhere else can you find five people with completely different backgrounds united by a shared belief that every loss is somebody else's fault.  A typical Dota 2 match consists of:  One guy who thinks he's the next world champion. One guy playing music through his microphone. One guy whose internet is powered by a hamster. One guy who hasn't spoken all game but suddenly writes a 500-word essay explaining why everyone else is terrible. And you, wondering why you didn't just watch a movie instead.  The game is so addictive that players will spend 60 minutes suffering through the most miserable match imaginable, swear they'll never play again, close the game, stare at the desktop for 30 seconds, and immediately queue for another match.  The heroes themselves are absurd. Some are gods, dragons, demons, and ancient cosmic entities capable of destroying worlds. Yet all of them can be beaten to death by a shirtless guy with an axe who runs at people screaming.  The matchmaking system deserves its own award. Somehow it consistently creates games where both teams are convinced they received the worse players.  Dota 2 isn't a game.  It's a relationship.  A toxic, emotionally damaging relationship that occasionally gives you one incredible victory just to keep you from leaving.  And that's why millions of players keep coming back.  Because deep down, every Dota player believes the next game will be different.  It won't.  Queue accepted. 🎮💀

Not so good, not so bad.  500 hours in, I can tangibly see something has changed. I can hear my tone of voice as if I have a separate conscience saying "whoa there buddy chill out" but I cant because why the phuck would you buy dagger for Anti Mage?    1000 hours in. I feel it. I go out once a week. I now work from home, playing in-between tasks. My wife is worried about me. She's saying she doesn't know me anymore. My daughter is scared.    1500 hours in. 3AM screams at night, worse than nightmare 'cause here I am not dreaming. CM first item desolator and slark is feeding. I don't know what to do anymore but I push through.    2000 hours. I reached the ungodly amount of immersing myself within the game itself. It's as if I am living an endless cycle of personal nightmare manifested every minute I hold the mouse. It's as if the conscience inside me farmed and bought Manta just to ♥♥♥♥ with me every now and then.    I sleep but never rest. I’ve been playing Dota since 2007, back in the Warcraft III days, long before it became what it is now.    Over the years, I had the chance to play with and against many of the biggest names from the early Dota scene. I met a lot of friends through this game, followed its evolution, and watched it grow from custom lobbies into one of the greatest esports titles in the world.    I haven’t actively played for the last 4–5 years, but Dota is still a huge part of my life. I still watch every major tournament, follow the teams, the players, the stories, the drama, and the history behind it all. This game shaped a big part of my youth, my friendships, my competitive mindset, and even the way I think about games.    This summer I’m flying to Shanghai for The International, and honestly, that says everything. Even after all these years, Dota still matters to me.    It’s more than just a game. It’s a world I grew up in.

I approached Dota 2 with genuine interest, hoping to understand its celebrated complexity through Turbo matches—a supposedly lower-stakes environment. Instead, I was met with relentless toxicity. Despite making honest efforts to learn, I was spam-reported simply for not playing perfectly. The result? A communications ban, an 8-hour matchmaking suspension, and even an Overwatch penalty—all for the crime of being inexperienced.  The community doesn’t teach; it harasses. Every mistake is met with ping-spamming and verbal abuse. The automated systems don’t distinguish between intentional griefing and beginner struggles—they just weaponize mob rule against newcomers.  If Valve intends Dota 2 to have any future, this is a catastrophic failure. League of Legends, for all its flaws, offers a more constructive path to learning. It outclasses Dota in every meaningful way—approachability, community management, and basic respect for a player’s time.  As for Dota’s community: it deserves the same neglect Valve has shown Team Fortress 2—abandonment by developers, overrun by bots, and left to rot. Even that would be too kind.

If you get reported by a number of people, you get placed in low priority queue. This can happen in any game, even non-ranked for any reason. Someone doesn't like the way you play, items you picked, or your power went out and you left two separate games but even though you reconnected the damage was done.  Now in Low priority, you can only play single draft, which means you are not allowed to play any heroes you want, only select from a randomized 4 heroes. matchmaking times are longer, and to make matters worse, to get out of this punishment, you have to WIN BETWEEN 1-5 GAMES.  Imagine you are new, or coming back after years. Every game you get stomped, about 30minutes in and you know there is no coming back, they farm kills on everyone just for fun. Now imagine you are in a queue full of bad players, you yourself are bad at the game, and you cannot get out of the queue unless you get lucky. So your potentially stuck in this queue forEVER if you never win.  Do you see how stupid this is? they also took it a step further by taking away achievements, exp and anything gained for completing a match.  Every game in dota 2 is roughly an hour + long endeavour. This means you are committing to losing several matches, AND you don't get to play the heroes you want to play. How can a game be enjoyable with that sort of punishment?  This punishment is reserved for players with a string of reports, so like I said before, you are bad, you are losing, you are learning, but some sweaty, who belongs in RANKED, is playing all pick normal casual game and reporting you for not getting an item on a character. they are flaming you in chat and threatening to report you. So their toxic behavior benefits them, especially if they queue with friends that can mass report you.  I guess the lesson here is if you want to play dota 2, do not play any game besides the new player queue, and if you decided to go beyond that queue, make sure you queue with friends so you cannot be mass reported. If you do not have dota 2 friends, uninstall the game, and voice your complaints to gabe newell.

Its a game with a dangerous combination, its quite addicting but fundamentally broken at a root level, perhaps by design.  The game itself at its core is fun, the heroes are fun to play, and the game is updated semi-often (although the quality and quantity of said updates has significantly declined in the last few years).  Theirs no game quite like Dota 2, theirs over 100 heroes, all of them play completely differently, with their own skills and item builds, no two heroes feel the game. When playing with a stack theirs quite alot of thought that goes into your draft, your item build and your skill build. When it clicks its very addictive and quite honestly very fun. Nothing gives you a better dopamine hit than getting a rampage. Or winning that really close 50 minute game.   But good lord the games horribly designed, to begin with, this game is unbelievably complicated, staring at the screen, youll see numbers and graphics everywhere, even basic skills and abilities can have half a page of text explaining what they do, items can too.    This isnt a casual game that you play for a little while and move on, this is a game you play for life, or you dont play it at all. Even before you pick a hero to play, the fundamental basics of the game take months to learn, your looking at years of investment before your anywhere close to "good", thats just how complicated and in-depth this game is.   If you want to play a new hero for example, youll need to read guides, watch videos and replays. You might be ok on a hero initially, but without going off and doing a bunch of research, you'll just end up throwing games and having people rage at you, this isnt a game, its a second job, in most cases it feels like your primary job.   Then theirs the large patches that come out every 2-3 months, Dota 2 patches can be quite big. Dozens of pages and notes affecting huge amounts of heroes, items and even the map. Every time a release comes out you may have your favourite item or hero completely reworked entirely, meaning you effectively have to learn them again every 6-12 months, depending on the hero. Even worse, some patches completely change the basics of the game so drastically it feels like your playing a whole new game, this happens often.    But perhaps the biggest design flaw of this game is the way it handles advantages in the early and mid game. When you get kills, you get money and experience, same with towers. Money lets you buy items to get stronger, experience helps you level up so you can get more stats and abilities. If one team gets an early lead, assuming a somewhat equal draft, then coming back is almost impossible since just being ahead gives you an advantage so you can get even more ahead, Dota 2 is an EXTREMELY snowball heavy game, once the snowball starts rolling, stopping it becomes almost impossible.   The old saying "whoever gets ahead stays ahead" should be the motto of this game. In the rare case you do manage to pull off a comeback, its because the enemy team has made a series of major mistakes to let your team back in, rarely is it because you “outplayed” anyone.  This snowball methodology becomes so laughable that even if you do outplay the other team, you often wont get a kill just because the enemy team is so tanky, and your own items are so far behind that kills becomes significantly more difficult. The game actively prohibits better teams winning if they lose the mid/eary game because they are just too far behind to catchup. Cores with a gold/experience lead can legitimately wipe 3-4 enemy heroes on their own.   Think of it this way, your playing tennis and you get a break of serve, your now ahead, as a result of you getting that break of serve, your own serve is now 25% faster, you move 25% quicker and your 25% less tired. This is the basic premise in Dota 2, you get a significant advantage just for being ahead, this happens in no other sport, you dont get to move 25% faster just because you sunk a three pointer in basketball. You dont get to throw a pitch 25% faster because you just struck someone out previously in a game of baseball.   Moreover, stomps are VERY common in this game. 4/5 games are stomps where you know whose going to win after the first 10 or so minutes, where Dota 2 shines is that 1/5 game that goes down to the wire and you win. The addiction comes when you try to chase that same feeling and game for the next 3 days in vain.   The other critical flaw in this game is just how minimal you can feel in terms of impact. You are 1 of 10 players, so even though you may be playing a great game, it doesnt matter if two other people on your team are just playing for laughs and giggles and end up dying alot in the early part of the game. Conversely, youll often win games where you play terribly, because someone else in your team is stomping everyone else. The only role that is absolutely critical is the position 1 role, you can win games with bad supports or an average mid, but you wont win with a terrible position 1. This often means that support players often feel like wins are out of their hands, it can be incredibly deflating when your playing fantastic as a support, but your position 1 feed 5 kills in the opening 2 minutes.   The other aspect of this game that often gets overlooked is just how toxic games can be. This isnt COD or BF2 where games last 15 minutes, you can leave whenever you want and no one really talks because its just a goofy, fun game where no one really cares that much. Dota 2 games can last 50 minutes, thats a long time to be stuck with the same 4 people on your team, if things are going badly, people can get toxic very quickly, and you cant leave or quit before the game ends  since theres no surrender button. If your behind as per my above point, comebacks just dont happen that often, and as a result its not uncommon to be sitting in a game that was over 15 minutes ago, just watching you and your team mates die over and over again for 20 minutes.   This game brings out the worst in people, you absolutely have to have very thick skin to play this game, its not for the timid or shy.   In the end, I have over 9000 hours in this game, most of the time I have not enjoyed, but due to how addicting it can be, stopping becomes challenging. If you do take the plunge and dedicate serious amounts of time and effort into this game, quitting becomes even harder because youve already dedicated a huge amount of time already, time you dont want to feel like youve "wasted".   I often think about what I could have done with those 9000 hours, what skills I could have developed or interests I could have pursued. Dont be like me, I hate this game and I wish I never played it.

I want to like Dota 2. I want to. Really. But my experience boils down to (200+hours of) frustration...and to this:  1) Queue times are in advance of 8 minutes.   2) Sorry ~ its a team game and im connecting to the US- West and East only servers ~ why are 30+% of my games 100% lobies of Eastern European players? I'd like to be able to communicate. ♥♥♥♥♥ lame.  3) Statistically 30+% of my games have a leaver within the first 5 minutes... so you have to re-queue. (see point 1)  4) The sweat is so insanely off the charts... get ready for the chat spam.  5) If you're new, simply forget playing anything other than 'respawn simulator'. I *dont* wanna hear "go check out the tutorials" ~ Didnt do that with Civ 5, LoL, the C&C series, the AoE series ~ you name it ~ when a game has THIS MUCH thrown at the end-user and ♥♥♥♥ STILL feels like you need a textbook to play it ~ guess what? ~ The game is NEEDLESSLY convoluted. 200+ hours feel *wasted* when the end-user doesnt get any better or learn.   6) This beats on the 'not-noobie-friendly' door but seriously having heros such an extremely small pool of "non-complex" heros doesnt help. It really only pushes people out. The extreme amount of map knowledge and the extreme amount of hero's that can REVOKE that knowledge or make it mean ♥♥♥♥-all makes for an extremely frustrating learning curve. You have grabs that can reach insanely far (Pudge), you have teliports, dashes, straight up going through terrain. Name it ~ it means learning the map feels ♥♥♥♥♥♥ useless and daunting.   7) Vision. ♥♥♥♥ on a stick ~ I get it ~ shadows and elevation and ♥♥♥♥... if #5 stands then placing wards straight up feels like a waste of gold.  8) You do you but I feel the UI is a mess. Dont get me wrong, the information is *there*. But again.... to a new player its an absolute bombardment of menu's and mouse-over's for information that gets overlapped 1000000 times by asterisks. For crying out loud the mouse-over menu's include recent patch notes... like.... ffs clean this ♥♥♥♥ up.

Until 2022, it was an excellent game.  After 2022, it has been in constant decline.  Constant small and major “updates” keep making the game worse.  Matchmaking The matchmaking randomness is insane — the chance of winning or losing feels like 50/50. You can lose 10 matches in a row, then win 10 in a row. Your personal skill has almost no impact on the outcome.  There is a constant feeling that you have no control over the game, even if you receive the “Best Player of the Match” title more often than other players.  Hack-and-slash The game has turned from a strategy game into a hack-and-slash. From “play smart” to “play as a crowd.” Victory no longer goes to the more skillful player, but to the team that can bring more allies to a fight at the right moment. You have become COMPLETELY dependent on your teammates.  The International 2025 This led to The International 2025 becoming the longest tournament in the game's history. The best players of the world - Teams were so afraid to take fights without all five players present that five matches turned into around ten team fights stretched across five hours. Even the commentators did not seem happy, because there was almost nothing happening in the game to talk about.  Play every day Every “update” drastically changes several random heroes. So if you don’t play every day, but only once a week, you will constantly be unpleasantly surprised when your hero suddenly dies for no apparent reason. The reason is simple: you were not prepared for the “new mechanic” introduced in the latest weekly update. And yes, you will have to search YouTube for information about the “new updates” just to understand why your hero died. The experience and mastery you accumulated with a character become meaningless because of the constant radical changes introduced in weekly “updates.”

The game gets updated — but that's where Valve's effort ends. Behind the polished patches and shiny new heroes lies a company that has completely abdicated its responsibility to the player base. Smurfs run rampant with no consequence, the matchmaking punishes honest players, and the reporting system is essentially decorative. Valve collects billions from this community through cosmetics and battle passes, yet can't be bothered to enforce basic standards of fair play. At this point, the gameplay is being held together by the developers, while the community is being left to rot by management. That's not a game with problems — that's a game being actively mismanaged by people who stopped caring a long time ago.  The good: Constant patches and evolving metas keep the game fresh and balanced. Genuinely commendable.  The bad: -The ranking and matchmaking system is a joke. Hit a win streak, then get thrown into an unwinnable losing spree packed with smurfs and toxic players. Which I strongly believe they do it on purpose - otherwise it will be impossible to explain this suffering from this loop many times. Escaping this loop alone is nearly impossible — and smurfs face zero real consequences. Many openly admit buying accounts and have never been penalized once.  -The communication system is absurd. You get provoked, stay silent, and your communication score drops anyway. You end up muted or restricted regardless.

The behavior score system is completely broken. After 15 games without receiving a single report, I only gained +280 behavior score. But after getting 9 reports in just 2 games — including 5 reports from a full enemy stack — I received a low priority penalty and lost -1200 score.  At this point, it feels less like a game and more like a report-abuse simulator. Players are forced to grind endlessly for tiny behavior score gains, while coordinated reports instantly destroy your progress. Meanwhile, account buyers, smurfs, and cheaters can freely play on 12k behavior score accounts because they do not care if the account gets ruined or banned.  The current system punishes normal players far more than actual offenders.

Dota 2 is basically the lesser known, more tactical, strategic brother of League of Legends. Some of the key differences that I noticed is that every single hero is free from the start, and that it plays VERY differently from other traditional Moba types.  There are still three lanes, top middle and bottom, but the jungle is huge compared to league, and there isn't a 'jungle' roll. There's so many different nuanced gimmicks and tricks that you're always able to learn something. Compared to league of legends, Dota 2 is far more engaging with how you can gain the upper hand. Rather than dodging skillshots and landing combos like in league of legends, Dota 2 is more focused on making decisions. - Do I fight here? - Do I buy this item? - Is this place warded? - What's my Safe Lane's 'Timing'? - Do I pull the wave, or stack this camp? Maybe I can do both? And most importantly: - Which hero would make the other team's hero composition worthless?  Getting the right draft is half the battle in Dota 2. In league of legends, most of the champions are quite similar with how they play. Usually just: "His Q is in a cone and slows, while her Q is in a line and stuns"  In Dota 2, the heroes are WAY more unique. Their strengths would make them absolutely unfair in League of Legends, and their weaknesses are so glaring it makes them seem worthless. It's all about the situation that hero finds themself in.  - For instance, there's a hero named 'Silencer' who's ult is, guess this, a global silence for over 3 seconds.  - Then there's Rubick, who's whole playstyle is stealing abilities from other heroes and using stronger versions of them. - There's Meepo, who makes clones of himself and controls them all like it's an RTS game, and he can teleport between all the other clones. Under attack? Teleport to a clone who's farming a camp. Attacking? Make ALL the other clones teleport to you, and jump their support with a combined root time of about 6 seconds. - Windranger can run super fast, make all attacks miss her, and with the right item, also be invisible while pelting you with attacks at a super high rate. She can also stun you for three seconds, and deal huge damage with a power shot from half the map. By the way, the move speed buff and attack immunity is a basic ability. It's duration is almost as long as it's cooldown, meaning she's more often immune to attacks than not.  There are so many examples of heroes having crazy abilities that would make them completely unstoppable if dropped into a League game.  The lore is LSD in writing, don't bother trying to understand it.  Dota 2 honestly plays more like a stealth game. vision is so incredibly important, that if no one places, or gets rid of wards. You WILL lose.  So why did I not recommend the game?  Because like League, Dota 2 is a toxic, horribly addicting, rage inducing, life ruining game that's filled with griefers, boosters, and moronic animals that get you killed then say "No support noob inting jajajajaja"  Oh yeah, and don't expect anyone to understand you even in US west servers, because for some reason the cartel doesn't like to play with low ping.  If you ragequit League Of Legends and are looking to play something different, don't try Dota 2 because it will end the same. Just play Noita instead.
